concepts.title: Cloud Formation
concepts.description: Clouds form when water vapor in the atmosphere condenses into visible liquid droplets or ice crystals. This process occurs when the air cools to its dew point, causing the water vapor to change state from gas to liquid or solid.

concepts.title: Types of Clouds
concepts.description: Clouds are classified into three main types: Cirrus, Cumulus, and Stratus. Cirrus clouds are high-level clouds that appear as thin, wispy lines. Cumulus clouds are puffy, white clouds that can appear alone or in large clusters. Stratus clouds are low-level clouds that cover the sky like a blanket.

concepts.title: Evaporation and Condensation
concepts.description: Evaporation is the process by which water is transformed from a liquid to a gas. Condensation is the process by which water vapor in the air is transformed back into a liquid, forming clouds and precipitation.

concepts.title: Water Cycle Mnemonic
concepts.description: Use the mnemonic 'E V A P O R A T E' to remember the water cycle: Evaporation, Vaporization, Ascension, Precipitation, Orogenesis, Runoff, Accumulation, and Transpiration.

concepts.title: Confusing Evaporation and Transpiration
concepts.description: Many students confuse evaporation and transpiration. Evaporation is the process by which water is transformed from a liquid to a gas, while transpiration is the process by which plants release water vapor into the air through their leaves.

concepts.title: Units of Measurement for Precipitation
concepts.description: To report precipitation, use the following units: millimeters (mm), centimeters (cm), inches (in), or liters per square meter (L/m2).

concepts.title: Role of Water in the Atmosphere
concepts.description: Water plays a crucial role in the Earth's atmosphere, regulating temperature and weather patterns through the water cycle and influencing the formation of clouds and precipitation.

concepts.title: Importance of Clouds
concepts.description: Clouds are essential for the Earth's climate, providing shade, blocking harmful radiation, and influencing precipitation patterns.
